data.title: Upbeat Earnings Send S&P 500, Nasdaq to New Highs — WSJ
data.description:
By Roshan Fernandez

The S&P 500 and Nasdaq composite rose to fresh records on Thursday after upbeat earnings from some major companies lifted investors' optimism that profits can weather tariffs and shifting trade policy.

PepsiCo shares gained 7.4% after the company posted better-than-expected earnings. United Airlines added 3.1% after it predicted a stronger second half of the year. And Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing climbed 3.4% in the U.S. after the world's largest contract chip maker posted a record quarterly profit.

"These are all tariff-sensitive companies, and they've had all good earnings, good guidance," said Gina Bolvin, president of Bolvin Wealth Management Group. "You add it all up and it's just good news for stocks."

The S&P added 0.5% on Thursday to close at its ninth record of the year, and the tech-heavy Nasdaq gained 0.7% to hit its fourth consecutive record, the longest such streak since November. The Dow Jones Industrial Average rose about 230 points, or 0.5%, to 44484.49.

United's new outlook remains lower than its original forecast for this year. But the airline said demand started to accelerate earlier this month. The broader market, and businesses, now have a better read on how they'll manage tariffs, Chief Executive Scott Kirby said.

"Encouragingly, that higher level of certainty has translated into a meaningful inflection point in demand," Kirby said on a call with investors.

GE Aerospace raised its earnings outlook and beat analysts' expectations on its top and bottom lines. Shares fell 2%.

The results helped investors look past President Trump's most-recent tariff threats. Trump said Wednesday that he plans to set a blanket unilateral tariff rate, which could be 10% or 15%, on goods from more than 150 nations.

Investors also welcomed better-than-expected retail sales and weekly jobless claims on Thursday. Sales rose 0.6% in June, largely reversing a drop from May and suggesting that tariff-driven price increases and uncertainty aren't deterring shoppers.

That might be because demand was pulled forward and built-up inventories haven't run out yet, said Charlie Ripley, portfolio manager at Allianz Investment Management, but the top-line figure was reassuring.

"Clearly, the retail sales numbers [are] showing that consumers still have that wherewithal to spend," Ripley said. "And so in that vein, I think we feel pretty good about where the economy is sitting today."

The number of Americans who filed for unemployment benefits was down last week, the Labor Department said Thursday. Filings fell to 221,000, fewer than the 234,000 claims that economists polled by The Wall Street Journal were anticipating.

"I don't think we have too much of a worry in terms of what the labor market outlook is looking like," Ripley said.

The dollar extended its rebound after Wednesday's roller-coaster day, which included a series of reports about the possibility that Trump could attempt to fire Federal Reserve Chair Jerome Powell.

Former Fed governor Kevin Warsh said on CNBC that he believes the next Fed chair will maintain the central bank's independence, even amid Trump's political pressure to slash interest rates. Warsh is among the candidates Trump is considering to replace Powell when his term expires next year.

Federal Reserve governor Adriana Kugler said Thursday morning that the Fed should hold interest rates steady, pointing to likely inflation stemming from new tariffs in the months ahead. That stance, which echoed Wednesday's comments by New York Fed President John Williams, runs contrary to Trump's push for rate cuts.

Write to Roshan Fernandez at roshan.fernandez@wsj.com

data.title: Norfolk Southern Shares Rise on WSJ Report of Union Pacific Acquisition Interest
data.description:
By Nicholas G. Miller

Shares of Norfolk Southern rose in late trading after The Wall Street Journal reported that Union Pacific was in early-stage talks to buy its smaller rival.

Norfolk Southern's stock was recently up 4% in after-hours trading to $280.60. The stock was up 17% to $269.81 over the past 12 months through Thursday's close.

Shares of Union Pacific, meanwhile, fell about 1.1% to $225.01, after closing the regular trading session at $227.49.

The Wall Street Journal report said the talks weren't guaranteed to result in a deal or receive regulatory approval and that another buyer could also emerge.

Write to Nicholas G. Miller at nicholas.miller@wsj.com.

data.title: Netflix Earnings Soar Past Expectations. How a Weak Dollar Helped Boost Results. — Barrons.com
data.description:
By Adam Levine

Ginny & Georgia streamer Netflix beat expectations for its second-quarter earnings results Thursday afternoon. Its shares were down in after-hours trading.

This is breaking news. Read a preview of Netflix's earnings below and check back for more analysis soon.

When Netflix reports its second quarter on Thursday afternoon, the Ginny & Georgia streamer will look to keep up a hot streak that began at the end of 2023. Wall Street analysts are expecting earnings per share of $7.08, up from $4.88 last year, and revenue of $11.1 billion, up 16%.

After its sales growth slowed in 2022, Netflix pulled two levers to turn things around. It cracked down on password-sharing and began offering a less expensive ad-supported tier.

The initiatives were successful, and now Netflix has notched six quarters in a row with double-digit revenue growth. The stock price has reflected that, up 129% since Netflix reported the first of those quarters in January 2024. The S&P 500 index was up 28% in the same period.

This run has stretched the stock's valuation, trading at 44 times expected earnings for the next 12 months, close to its three-year high. Other valuation metrics are similarly inflated. To keep the rally going, Netflix can't show any weakness in the second quarter, or in its guidance for the third quarter, when analysts expect earnings per share of $6.69 on $11.3 billion in revenue.

For Netflix to keep up, the ad tier will have to continue its rapid growth. According to Melissa Otto, the head of Visible Alpha Research at S&P Global, it saw $1.9 billion in 2024 sales and is expected to get $3.9 billion this year, making it an important driver for the whole company.

Ads may also be more profitable than subscriptions. The company's operating profit margin has been growing, last seen at 32% in the first quarter, and the streamer projected 33% for this quarter.

"Netflix has built a formidable entertainment platform and is in the early stages of developing a promising digital advertising franchise," said analyst Brian White of Monness, Crespi, Hardt in a note to clients. "We expect Netflix to enjoy continued momentum on the digital advertising front, benefit from higher monthly subscription prices, and release a steady flow of new content."

Netflix has survived an onslaught of new competition in the past few years from Disney, Amazon, Apple and more. According to Nielsen's June U.S. viewing data, Netflix easily tops all but Alphabet's YouTube, whose viewing share has increased from 9.9% a year ago to 12.8% in June, with Netflix trailing with 8.3%. The next closest is the combination of Disney's three streaming services at a 4.8% share.

Increasingly, Netflix squares off with YouTube while the rest compete with each other. The wild card is TikTok, which may become a larger part of the picture down the road.

Netflix gets a majority of its revenue from abroad, so the weak dollar in the second quarter could be a tailwind.

Write to Adam Levine at adam.levine@barrons.com

This content was created by Barron's, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. Barron's is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

data.title: Consolidated Edison Keeps Quarterly Dividend at $0.85 per Share, Payable Sept. 15 to Holders of Record Aug. 13
F.N.B. Corp. Q2 Non-GAAP EPS, Revenue Increase
data.description:
F.N.B. Corp. reported Q2 non-GAAP earnings Thursday of $0.36 per diluted share, up from $0.34 a year earlier.

Analysts polled by FactSet expected $0.34.

Revenue for the quarter, expressed as the sum of net interest income and total noninterest income, was $438.2 million, up from $403.8 million a year earlier.

Analysts surveyed by FactSet expected $424.2 million.

data.title: Western Alliance Bancorporation Names Vishal Idnani As Next Chief Financial Officer >WAL
Tech, Media & Telecom Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Technology, Media and Telecom. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

1639 ET - Netflix's international content is resonating with its users. The streaming service said non-English language series represented more than one-third of all Netflix viewing in the first half of the year. "Even with our 2025 slate being unusually second-half weighted, our members watched over 95 billion hours on Netflix in the first half of the year, a 1% increase year over year," the company said in a letter to shareholders. (denny.jacob@wsj.com; @pennedbyden)

1634 ET - Netflix can thank a weaker dollar for its raised outlook. The streaming service raised its revenue forecast to $44.8 billion to $45.2 billion for the year, adding that the majority of the increase is due to the recent depreciation of the dollar against most other currencies. Momentum in member growth and ad sales rounds out its higher guidance. Shares tick down 0.5% in after hours trading. (denny.jacob@wsj.com; @pennedbyden)

0834 ET - Publicis Chairman and CEO Arthur Sadoun gives little weight to the threat for the industry posed by Meta Platforms' AI ad offering. Meta's plans have emerged as a top concern among investors in ad groups since The Wall Street Journal reported last month that the Facebook owner aims to fully automate ad creation using AI. "It's eight years today that I'm presenting results and I have been hearing that platform will eat us for breakfast basically since I started them. And since then, we have actually doubled our revenue and more than doubled our market cap," Sadoun says in an earnings call. Shares in Publicis fall 5.8%. (adria.calatayud@wsj.com)

0517 ET - South Korean talent agency HYBE, the label behind global K-pop boy band BTS, may be better positioned than its local peers to monetize the fan-engagement platform, Nomura analysts Angela Hong and Won Kang write in a note. The entertainment company has aggressively expanded its Weverse platform, with monthly active users surpassing 10 million and expected to continue growing, they say. The analysts see the platform integrating content, subscriptions, merchandise sales, commerce and livestreaming in a single stack. All seven BTS members have recently completed their mandatory military service in South Korea, raising hopes that they will reunite soon and perform as a group again after a multiyear hiatus. (kwanwoo.jun@wsj.com)

0458 ET - Tele2's low debt level raises questions about potential payouts, Berenberg analysts write in a note. The Swedish telecom company delivered a second-quarter performance ahead of consensus on Ebitda after leases, driven by strong cost savings, and raised its full-year guidance, they note. While shares aren't cheap, momentum is accelerating as savings boost Ebitdaal growth, they say. The German brokerage expects Tele2 to benefit from any future sector consolidation. Shares are up 7.9% at 147.25 Swedish kronor. (najat.kantouar@wsj.com)

0444 ET - India's Tech Mahindra likely faces a tough outlook after its 1Q Ebitda came in line with expectations and revenue missed estimates, say Citi analysts in a note, highlighting concerns of increased market fragmentation and macro uncertainty. Companies in the sector "expanding margins meaningfully in the current environment is a tough ask--even from a low base," they write. Citi cites a tough industry backdrop, noting that it expects flattish revenues on-year in FY 2026. Citi maintains a sell rating on the stock and lowers its target price to INR1,400 from INR1,430. Shares are 2% lower at INR1,575.40. (megan.cheah@wsj.com)

0421 ET - Nvidia shares edge higher Thursday premarket after Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Co. posted a strong set of second-quarter results. Nvidia stock closed 0.4% higher at $171.37 on Wednesday. Shares are up 0.9% at $172.98 Thursday premarket. TSMC's quarterly net profit jumped 61% to 398.27 billion New Taiwan dollars, beating a NT$379.415 billion FactSet estimate. Revenue grew 39% to NT$933.79 billion, topping company guidance. The world's largest contract chip maker produces chips for clients like Nvidia and Apple. Better-than-expected results show that demand for chips to power the artificial-intelligence boom remains strong despite tariff uncertainty. (mauro.orru@wsj.com)

0359 ET - European chip stocks rise in early morning trade after solid results from Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing signal demand for chips stays strong. The Hsinchu, Taiwan company posted a 61% rise in second-quarter profit that beat consensus expectations and guided for third-quarter sales of between $31.8 billion and $33.0 billion, ahead of the $30.07 billion it made in the second quarter. Dutch maker of semiconductor equipment ASML rises 2.5%, while smaller peers ASM International and BE Semiconductor Industries gain 3.3% and 3.5%, respectively. STMicroelectronics rises 3.4% while German rival Infineon Technologies advances 1.4%. France's Soitec is up by 2.9% while Switzerland's VAT Group and Germany's Aixtron rise 3.2% and 3.8%, respectively.(maitane.sardon@wsj.com)

0308 ET - Baidu's partnership with Uber highlights its leading position in autonomous-driving technology and is a positive development for the Chinese company, Jefferies analysts say. They note that thousands of Apollo Go autonomous vehicles will be deployed in various markets outside the U.S. and China, starting with Asia and the Middle East later this year. Baidu's leadership in the space is the result of more than a decade of investment in the technology and its pursuit of different business models to scale up, they write in a note. Jefferies maintains a buy rating on the stock with a target price of $110.00. Its ADRs last closed at $86.32. (jiahui.huang@wsj.com; @ivy_jiahuihuang)

2336 ET - The market is likely overly bullish on NetEase's 2Q results after the strong 1Q beat, Nomura analysts say in a research note. They project 2Q revenue for NetEase's online gaming and value-added services segment to have risen 12% on year, below consensus expectations for 17% growth. Revenue growth for NetEase's 2Q PC gaming revenue may have slowed to a forecast 62% from 1Q's 85% due to normalized performance of the two recent new titles, "Marvel Rivals" and "Where Winds Meet," the analysts say. However, NetEase's margins likely improved further due to expanded gross profit margin for the nongaming business, they add. Nomura maintains a buy call on NetEase and raises its H-share target price to HK$242.00 from HK$233.00. Shares are last at HK$208.20. (sherry.qin@wsj.com)

2251 ET - Tencent's 2Q profit growth was likely dented by its AI investment, Nomura analysts say in a research note. Tencent's online gaming revenue likely rose 15% on year in 2Q thanks to the resilient performance of its legacy titles like "Honor of Kings" and some recently released titles like "Delta Force," the analysts say. Its online ads revenue likely grew 18%, driven by video account ads, they say. Despite higher AI spending, Nomura thinks Tencent's adjusted operating margin should be able to sustain its expansion streak. Nomura maintains a buy rating on Tencent with its target price unchanged at HK$670.00. Shares are last 0.6% lower at HK$513.50. (sherry.qin@wsj.com)

2212 ET - Baidu's ad revenue could remain under pressure in 2H, Citi analysts say in a note. Amid the tech giant's fast AI search ramp up and muted ad-spending sentiment, ad revenue's drag could be bigger than Citi's previous expectations. The bank lowers Baidu's core ad revenue forecast by 6.1% for 2Q and 8.9% for 3Q. Given bigger negative impact from the higher-margin ad revenue, the analysts also lower Baidu's core operating profit estimates by 21% for 2Q and 24% for 3Q. Citi maintains a buy call on Baidu and raises its ADR target price to US$140.00 from US$138.00. ADRs last ended at US$86.32. (sherry.qin@wsj.com)

data.title: Health Care Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering the Health Care sector. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

1455 ET - For manufacturing construction companies, such as Jacob Solutions and Fluor, EV plants are out and GPL-1 factories are in. Multibillion EV and semiconductor projects are close to being completed, but investment by pharmaceutical companies, boosted by huge demand for weight loss drugs, could fill the upcoming revenue void for the construction industry, say UBS analysts in a research note. The analysts estimate $280 billion committed by pharmaceutical companies to manufacturing construction in the next four to five years, which would add 0.5% to 1% growth in both 2026 and 2027 to non-residential construction spending. The investments will help pharma companies avoid tariffs while ramping up production of weight loss drugs. (nicholas.miller@wsj.com)

0953 ET - Germany's chemical and pharmaceutical industry doesn't expect an upturn until 2026, according to industry association VCI. The association says the rapid decline of recent years has halted despite economic and geopolitical challenges, though there will be no growth this year. Capacity utilization has improved but remains below the threshold for profitability, VCI says. A lack of orders is one of the biggest complaints of the association's member companies. VCI represents around 2,300 companies. In 2024, VCI member companies generated a turnover of around 240 billion euros. (sarah.sloat@wsj.com)

0735 ET - Novartis CEO Vas Narasimhan says Europe needs to reform how it sets drug prices, or risk losing access to more innovative medicines. "Europe needs to do a better job rewarding innovative medicines," Narasimhan says in a call with reporters after the Swiss drugmaker's second-quarter results. Low prices at launch have already led to some medicines not being launched in Europe or being delayed, and the number will likely increase over time unless conditions change, he says. Shares fall 0.6%. (adria.calatayud@wsj.com)

0731 ET - Novartis has no plans to change its footprint in Switzerland or the rest of Europe as it bolsters its presence in the U.S., CEO Vas Narasimhan says. The Swiss drugmaker expects to keep its European footprint stable in coming years, without pursuing expansion plans, Narasimhan says in a call with reporters after the company's second-quarter earnings. Novartis in April promised to invest $23 billion in the U.S. over the next five years to expand its presence. A trade war isn't productive for either continent's economies or for the pharmaceutical industry and a free flow of innovative medicines is in best interest of all countries, he says. (adria.calatayud@wsj.com)

0726 ET - Novartis is confident its plan to expand its footprint in the U.S. over the medium term will allow it to fully mitigate any hit from tariffs, CEO Vas Narasimhan says in an earnings call. The Swiss drugmaker in April pledged to invest $23 billion to expand its presence in the U.S. over the next five years. Narasimhan says the plan seeks to produce key medicines in the U.S. for the local market over the medium term, which would fully mitigate any impact from tariffs the Trump administration plans to introduce. Moving drug production from one location to another typically takes three to four years, Narasimhan says. (adria.calatayud@wsj.com)

0649 ET - Sarepta's strategic review and planned reduction of 36% of its workforce are likely necessary for the company to keep up with debt and rising sales concerns, Cantor analysts Kristen Kluska and Richard Miller say. The biopharmaceutical company is still on the hook for $1.15 billion in convertible notes by 2027, making the expected $400 million in cost savings an important boost. The analysts also expect Sarepta's Elevidys product to hit some rocky demand after two patient deaths. Sarepta did get FDA approval for a black box label on the product, but the analysts still have "deep concerns about the safety of the treatment." Shares jump 34% in pre-market trading. (katherine.hamilton@wsj.com)

0314 ET - Novartis's quarterly results exceeded expectations once again, but this might be overshadowed by news of the retirement of its well-regarded financial chief and concerns about psoriasis drug Cosentyx, Intro Health's Naresh Chouhan and Dominic Rose say. "Harry Kirsch, who is widely considered the best CFO in the industry by investors, is to retire," the analysts write in a research note. Moreover, Cosentyx sales missed expectations and only grew 6% at constant currency, the analysts say. The company called out rising competition in the U.S., slower growth elsewhere and price cuts in the Chinese market, but remains confident in a peak annual sales target of $8 billion, the analysts say. Shares fall 2.6%. (adria.calatayud@wsj.com)

0302 ET - Novartis reported impressive results, extending a stretch of quarterly updates in which it beat expectations and raised guidance to a tenth quarter, Vontobel's Stefan Schneider says. The Swiss pharmaceutical company reported second-quarter core operating profit of $5.925 billion, up 21% at constant currency. This exceeded both consensus expectations and Vontobel's estimate, the analyst says in a research note. Novartis now expects core operating profit to grow by a low-teens percentage this year, having previously forecast an increase by low double digits. This guidance lift came as a surprise ahead of the anticipated arrival of generic competition for Novartis's heart drug Entresto--the group's best-selling medicine--in the U.S., Schneider says. Novartis shares closed at 95.12 Swiss francs on Wednesday. (adria.calatayud@wsj.com)

2350 ET - Jiangsu Hengrui Pharmaceuticals' revenue for the 2Q is expected to deliver growth of 12% to CNY8.5 billion, Nomura analysts write in a note. The brokerage estimates drugs sales registered 11.1% on-year growth in the 2Q to CNY7.1 billion, factoring in a continued ramp-up of newly launched drugs and expanded indications, they say. The company might deliver another one to two out-license deals, which could be the next catalysts for the stock, considering its broad R&D pipeline, they say. Nomura maintains a neutral rating for the stock and raises its target price to CNY59.15 from CNY53.45. Shares are at CNY57.46. (jiahui.huang@wsj.com; @ivy_jiahuihuang)

data.title: Financial Services Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Financial Services. Exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

1643 ET - Canadian consumer spending remained solid in 2Q, says Royal Bank of Canada economist Rachel Battaglia, based on anonymized cardholder data from the lender. This adds to a series of secondary and tertiary data that suggest the economy has not fallen off a cliff, as commentators and analysts feared due to President Trump's tariff threats. For the April-to-June period, core retail sales — which strips on automobiles and gasoline — rose 3%, based on activity on RBC credit and debit cards. Core retail sales did contract in June from May, according to RBC data, although Battaglia notes May was a particularly strong month. Quarterly spending growth remains positive in nearly all categories, Battaglia says, led by dining and entertainment. (paul.vieira@wsj.com; @paulvieira)

1411 ET - Elevance Health cut its guidance by slightly more than expected, but still within the realm of possibilities investors anticipated, Guggenheim analysts say in a note. Shares could be volatile given the updated outlook, but in the longer term, the new $30 2025 adjusted EPS baseline should give the stock a more attractive jump-off for next year, given the importance of health insurance exchange repricing and normalizing Medicaid costs for the company's long-term earnings growth target, the analysts say. Elevance slides 11%. (kelly.cloonan@wsj.com)

1354 ET - U.S. Bancorp's sale of $5.5 billion in consumer loans in 2Q weighed on lending profit margins and average earning assets, Edward Jones analyst James Shanahan says in a research note. An increase in residential mortgage paydowns also weighed on those assets and margins, the analyst says. Management is guiding for a modest recovery in lending income to start in 3Q, Shanahan says. "We remain focused on action and initiatives to strengthen net interest income, and those efforts are fully reflected in our guidance," CFO John Stern says on a call with analysts. Shares slide 2% to $44.76. (dean.seal@wsj.com)

1320 ET - The tone of Elevance Health's analyst call was even more cautious than expected, especially when it came to Medicaid, according to Mizuho in a note. The analysts say that although Medicaid managed care plans are required to receive actuarial sound rates, it could take two or three rate cycles for rates to fully capture current trends. This, plus uncertainty around the expiration of enhanced advanced premium tax credits and incremental Medicaid dis-enrollment, makes the company's earnings trajectory more unclear, the analysts say. Now, full margin recovery looks like it won't be until 2027 or 2028, they say. Elevance slides 11%. (kelly.cloonan@wsj.com)

1233 ET - Elevance Health says a slower-than-expected deceleration in rising Medicaid costs will hurt its bottom line this year, guiding for adjusted earnings per-share of about $30, compared to a midpoint of $34.50 in its previous range. "This reset reflects the same pressures that others in the sector have now confirmed," the company says during a call with analysts. Other health insurers, like UnitedHealth Group and Molina, have recently warned that high Medicare and Medicaid costs could pressure profits this year. Shares fall 11%.(kelly.cloonan@wsj.com)

0928 ET - Elevance says its cut to 2025 guidance is slightly more weighted toward issues in its Affordable Care Act business than Medicaid. The latest sign of medical-cost problems in the managed-care sector had been expected from the first big insurer to report earnings, but Elevance offered reassurance that costs in its Medicare business aren't running higher than predicted. In the ACA plans, the company pointed to high-cost patients signing up after losing Medicaid coverage, saying it was seeing higher emergency-room use, mental health care, and drug costs. Elevance is off 3% premarket. (anna.mathews@wsj.com)

0917 ET - U.S. Bancorp is the latest big bank to report signs of weakening net interest income. The U.S. Bank parent reported 2Q NII of $4.05 billion that missed Wall Street projections and net interest margin declined due to an elevated balance sheet and pricing pressure on deposits, Truist analysts say in a research note. The bank is issued soft 3Q guidance for NII, which will likely disappoint Wall Street, the analysts say. Earlier this week, Wells Fargo cut its NII guidance for the year due to lower interest income from its markets business. JPMorgan lowered its markets-related NII outlook as well. U.S. Bancorp shares slide 3.5% to $44.08 premarket. (dean.seal@wsj.com)

0321 ET - Wise PLC's underlying income was below expectations while its volume, cross-border revenue, take-rate and card revenue also came in softer, J.P.Morgan Cazenove says after the British money-transfer company's first-quarter results. "We expect shares to be slightly weaker today on the back of these results, especially after running up on the news of the UniCredit partnership earlier this week," analyst Craig A. McDowell writes in a research note. Shares trade down 8.8% shortly after market open at 1,032 pence, taking their year-to-date performance into the red. (elena.vardon@wsj.com)

0317 ET - EQT's half-year print justifies the strong run of the Swedish private equity firm's stock in recent weeks, Jefferies says in a research note. This is reflected in the group's realizations--the single most important proof point for the industry at the moment--where it saw 13 billion euros of gross fund in the six-month period, analysts write. This is more than the 11 billion euros it saw for all of 2024, they add. "Our tracker indicates there is a strong near-term pipeline of potential additional sales to add to that tally," they say. Shares rise 1.2% at market open. (elena.vardon@wsj.com)

0205 ET - The strong performance of U.S. investment banks is good news for European banks, which will report results in the coming weeks, Keefe, Bruyette & Woods say in a research note. The prints showed strong business performance as well as better-than-expected investment banking revenues on positive surprises in equity markets and M&A, the analysts write. Bullish commentary on the pipelines bodes well for the coming months, they add. This is supportive for European investment banks. However, the mixed share price reactions to the U.S. results confirm the need for relative caution since rising expectations may disappoint, they say. UBS and Barclays are well-placed into the results but BNP Paribas is less so, they add. (elena.vardon@wsj.com)

2031 ET - The risks to Tyro Payments from the Reserve Bank of Australia's proposal to scrap card-payment surcharges have been overstated, Morgans analyst Richard Coles reckons. He tells clients that the payment provider's management is adamant that the proposal won't have any short- or medium-term impact on its profit or margins. Coles writes in a note that Tyro is clearly better positioned than some other providers to handle the shifting regulations, and continues to have a large addressable market to grow into. Morgans keeps a buy rating and A$1.55 target price on the stock, which is up 1.8% A$0.9775. (stuart.condie@wsj.com)

data.title: Energy & Utilities Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Energy and Utilities. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

1518 ET - Oil futures rebound after three days of losses, supported by better-than-expected U.S. retail sales and a delayed response to yesterday's report of a 3.9 million barrel U.S. crude stock draw. The crude inventory data was overshadowed by large gasoline and diesel inventory builds. "The next move in the petroleum complex might be diesel generated," says Phil Flynn of the Price Futures Group, noting that even after last week's 4.2 million barrel build, stocks are 21% below the five-year average and at their lowest for the time of year in three decades. Nymex diesel futures rise 3.3% to a nearly one-month high. WTI settles up 1.7% at $67.54 a barrel and Brent gains 1.5% to $69.52. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

1256 ET - Oil futures press higher after a cautious start and look set to snap a three-day losing streak. Prices are supported by economic resilience in key oil consuming countries like the U.S. and China, and a resurgence of Middle East risk premium after drone attacks on oil infrastructure in Iraq's Kurdistan region, Pepperstone's Quasar Elizundia says in a note. "However, the market remains sensitive to trade policy and OPEC+ output decisions." WTI is up 1.6% at $67.46 as barrel and Brent rises 1.2% to $69.37.(anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

0920 ET - Crude oil futures are holding up after three days of moderate declines. Downward pressure on prices this week has been limited despite underlying concerns about increased output by OPEC+ and an eventual economic impact on demand from U.S. tariffs. "Obviously, as we progress to the latter half of the year, more supply is going to come into the market from OPEC, but ultimately I don't think demand is going to be as bad as had been feared," says Fawad Razaqzada of Forex.com. "There's going to be more sideways chop for the rest of the year than a trend emerging." WTI is up 0.3% at $66.59 a barrel, and Brent is flat at $68.52. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

0429 ET - SSE's first-quarter performance update was in line with expectations, analysts at J.P.Morgan write. Quarterly renewables output at the U.K. energy group was down 4% from last year due to unfavorable weather conditions. Consensus expectations for fiscal 2026 are likely to modestly decrease on the back of the lower renewables output, the analysts write. Overall, shares have significant potential to rise, JPM says. The analysts keep an overweight rating on the stock. Shares trade flat at 1844.5 pence.(adam.whittaker@wsj.com)

2118 ET - A path is clearing for Amplitude Energy to become a company worth at least A$1 billion, says Macquarie. Amplitude is raising utilization rates at its two natural-gas plants in Australia's Victoria state. That's happening at a time when the local gas market is tight. Amplitude is currently worth some A$623 million, with its shares up 2.2% at A$0.235 today. Macquarie says Amplitude needs to be trading at A$0.38/share to join the ranks of companies with a market value of A$1 billion. And that's exactly where the bank's 12-month price target sits. (david.winning@wsj.com; @dwinningWSJ)

2113 ET - Amplitude Energy's latest update should reassure investors about its growth plans, says Euroz Hartleys. Amplitude aims to develop the East Coast Supply Project at an estimated cost of A$380 million-A$455 million over FY 2026-FY 2028. Analyst Declan Bonnick says "the significantly improved Orbost production, now consistently operating at nameplate, should demonstrate to the market and provide belief that the company can fund" the ECSP project without needing new funds. Euroz Hartleys points to cash on hand of A$62.4 million, annual free cash flow generation of A$150 million, and existing debt facilities. "We argue there is little value for ECSP in the current share price, with the project set to double group revenue and triple earnings from FY 2024 levels," Euroz Hartleys says. (david.winning@wsj.com; @dwinningWSJ)

2046 ET - Citi says its timeline for first natural gas from Santos's Barossa project still looks plausible. It made the remarks after Santos said Barossa is now 97% complete, with the project undergoing final commissioning activities and progressing to plan. "Darwin LNG life extension activities seem on track and first gas from the asset unchanged, due this quarter, with our August start-up time looking likely," says analyst Paul McTaggart. Santos is up 0.1% at A$7.74. (david.winning@wsj.com; @dwinningWSJ)

data.title: Basic Materials Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Basic Materials. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

1403 ET - Concerns that President Trump may attempt to fire Federal Reserve chairman Jerome Powell have receded, deflating movement in gold and making it 3 out of the past 4 trading sessions that gold finishes lower. "Gold rallied on Wednesday on the back of speculation that President Trump was going to fire Fed chair Powell, but the air came out of that balloon as soon as Trump denied he was going to make his move, though he did confirm for the millionth time that he was not happy with the Fed Chair," says Robert Yawger of Mizuho Securities USA in a note. Movement ahead will have to do with the U.S. dollar turning stronger, says Yawger. Front-month gold closes down 0.4% to $3340.10 per troy ounce. (kirk.maltais@wsj.com)

0953 ET - Germany's chemical and pharmaceutical industry doesn't expect an upturn until 2026, according to industry association VCI. The association says the rapid decline of recent years has halted despite economic and geopolitical challenges, though there will be no growth this year. Capacity utilization has improved but remains below the threshold for profitability, VCI says. A lack of orders is one of the biggest complaints of the association's member companies. VCI represents around 2,300 companies. In 2024, VCI member companies generated a turnover of around 240 billion euros. (sarah.sloat@wsj.com)

0438 ET - Antofagasta delivered a decent second quarter and is poised to report a good set of first-half results in mid-August, Berenberg analysts write. The Chilean miner benefited from higher gold prices which caused second-quarter net cash costs to come in below expectations, they write. The company reported net cash costs of $1.12 a pound, compared with analyst expectations of $1.31 a pound. For the first half, they expect $3.8 billion in revenue and Ebitda of $2.2 billion. Net debt however could push up depending on when Antofagasta deploys its $3.9 billion of capital expenditure, they write. (adam.whittaker@wsj.com)

2334 ET - Rio Tinto's 2Q production result is "encouraging" given the miner's mixed record over the past five years, UBS analysts say in a note. "We see this as a solid base for incoming CEO Simon Trott to pursue his mandate to lift operational efficiency, streamline functions and simplify the business," they say. That said, they reckon "it won't be easy." The miner faces challenges in Mongolia, where it runs a giant copper mine, and in ramping up the huge Simandou iron-ore project in Guinea, which risks impacting the global iron-ore market. Rio Tinto also faces questions on its lithium strategy and overall portfolio mix, the analysts say. UBS trims its 2025 EPS estimate by 5%, citing the impact of tariffs. It keeps a neutral rating and A$115/share target. Rio Tinto is up 1.3% at A$111.92. (rhiannon.hoyle@wsj.com; @RhiannonHoyle)

2242 ET - Darma Henwa seems to be benefiting from structural transformation, UOB Kay Hian's Benyamin Mikael says in a research report. The coal miner is expanding in-house capacity, reducing subcontractor reliance and targeting third-party contracts, the analyst notes. This may drive a projected Ebitda CAGR of 42.1% over 2024-2028, the analyst says. The Indonesian company also owns a concession in Gayo Mineral Resources in Indonesia's Aceh. This marks strategic diversification beyond its core coal mining services, as the company seeks to tap copper and gold potential that could make substantial contributions. The brokerage initiates coverage of the stock with a buy rating and a target price of IDR372.00. Shares are 0.5% lower at IDR189.00. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

2239 ET - RBC Capital Markets reckons 29Metals posted "an OK result" for 2Q, with zinc output missing expectations but copper production in line. The miner's 2Q costs miss consensus estimates but matched RBC's expectations, the broker says in a note. "Importantly, costs are winding down at Capricorn, and cash balances went up." RBC has a sector perform rating and a A$0.30 target on the stock. "We await better cash flow generation from Golden Grove on grade and higher copper prices while remaining cognizant of the debt and upcoming capex," it says. 29Metals is down 10% at A$0.31. (rhiannon.hoyle@wsj.com; @RhiannonHoyle)

2104 ET - Jefferies continues to worry about 29Metals's balance sheet following its 2Q update. 29Metals's output missed the bank's expectations by some 16%, although this was partly offset by stronger revenue from its Golden Grove operation. 29Metals's share price is down 8.7% at A$0.315 because of the miss. "Operational earnings volatility continues to accentuate balance sheet uncertainty," analyst Daniel Roden says. Jefferies thinks positive pathways for improving operations are emerging. Still, the bank says "ongoing uncertainty on the timing and quantum of Capricorn Copper suspension and restart costs, debt servicing, and Gossan Hill capex obfuscates medium-term funding requirements." Jefferies retains a hold call on 29Metals. (david.winning@wsj.com; @dwinningWSJ)

2013 ET - Hansol Chemical could post a 2Q earnings beat on strong revenue growth led by its precursor business, Daiwa Capital analysts Henny Jung and Yoonki Bae write in a note. The South Korean company's operating profit is projected to have risen 37% on year to 48 billion won for the April-June quarter, likely above the market consensus forecast of 44 billion won, the analysts say. They expect Hansol's year-over-year profit growth to continue through 2027, driven by high-margin chemical products. Daiwa is upbeat on Hansol's commitment to return 20%-50% of its net income to shareholders and buy back 200,000 additional shares by August. (kwanwoo.jun@wsj.com)

1903 ET - Hopes of more Chinese steel-industry reforms and improving demand from the country's property sector are buoying iron-ore prices, says Commonwealth Bank of Australia analyst Vivek Dhar. However, Dhar says it's important to note that "the only true way to improve steel mill margins without hurting iron ore demand is by reducing China's outdated and unused steel capacity." Today that accounts for roughly 20%-25% of its capacity. "Given the year‑on‑year fall in China's steel output in May and June, it appears that steel output cuts is the policy direction," he says. As a result, iron-ore prices are likely to "eventually retrace lower," according to Dhar. Spot iron ore rose by 1.0% Wednesday, to US$99.10/metric ton. (rhiannon.hoyle@wsj.com; @RhiannonHoyle)

1834 ET [Dow Jones]--Alcoa says its strongest argument against the current 50% tariff on imported aluminum is the long odds of quickly adding more U.S. aluminum production. Alcoa has been supplying the U.S. market from smelters in Canada where electricity costs are more favorable for operating a smelter. The U.S. imports about four million metric tons of aluminum annually, the company says. "It would probably cost $30 billion to put four million metric tons here," CEO William Oplinger says. "It would probably take us at least five years. We're educating the government on those facts." (robert.tita@wsj.com)

1835 ET - President Trump's goal of bringing aluminum production to the U.S. may be difficult to achieve, said Alcoa Chief Executive William Oplinger in the company's 2Q earnings call. Building a new aluminum plant in the U.S. would take about five years, while replacing all of the U.S. aluminum imports with domestic production would take six gigawatts of energy and $30 billion, Oplinger said. He said he has met several times with top U.S. and Canadian officials to convince them of the challenges of moving production to the U.S. and the benefits for both economies of open trade between Canada and the U.S. (nicholas.miller@wsj.com)

1833 ET - Most of the aluminum Alcoa produces in Canada has to be shipped into the U.S. by contract, but still the company has flexibility over the destination of 30% of its Canadian production. Since March, the company has sold 100,000 metric tonnes of Canadian metal normally destined for the U.S. to non-U.S. customers. "At a 50% tariff, you saw us take action. We have the ability of 30% of the Canadian volume to redirect that to a non-U.S. designation" Chief Executive William Oplinger said on the company's earnings call. "And we will do so as long as the netbacks make more sense to ship it to other markets." (nicholas.miller@wsj.com)

data.title: Auto & Transport Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering the Auto and Transport sector. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

1238 ET - Business trips are back in business. United Airlines Chief Executive Scott Kirby says in an interview with CNBC that air travel among businesses has come back after a sluggish period around April, when many companies were holding off on expenses to see how tariffs would pan out. Business demand has accelerated by a double-digit percentage over the past three weeks, Kirby says in a call with analysts. "You see more and more businesses coming off the sidelines, getting unfrozen and I think getting back to business," he tells CNBC. United shares are up 1.2%. (katherine.hamilton@wsj.com)

1235 ET - United Airlines sees an opportunity to take more market share from low-cost carriers, who are suffering from cost convergence, Chief Executive Scott Kirby says on a call. Particularly at high-cost airports, low-cost carriers are dealing with rising expenses, while airlines like United and Delta have been able to operate more efficiently. "I look at every airline that's not named United or Delta. I can find at every single one of them a double-digit percentage of their route network that loses money," Kirby says. The only way for those carriers to improve margins would be to stop operating at money-losing routes, which opens market share for United. (katherine.hamilton@wsj.com)

1205 ET - United Airlines CEO Scott Kirby sees the U.S. economy stabilizing, which he believes is driving an uptick in air-travel demand over the past three weeks. Kirby says on a call with analysts that the passage of the budget reconciliation bill, along with easing tensions in the Middle East, are behind the six-point increase in demand United clocked in recent weeks. Even tariffs, though not yet set in stone, are creating fewer jitters as "the market and most businesses have a much better read on how they'll manage in a narrower range of outcomes," Kirby says. United shares rise 2.6%. (katherine.hamilton@wsj.com)

0504 ET - London-listed airlines are weighing on the FTSE 100, dragged by easyJet's profit warning. EasyJet reported an increase in earnings for its third quarter, but warned that profit growth for fiscal 2025 will be hit by higher fuel costs and the industrial action of French air traffic controllers. Shares in easyJet are down 6% at 4.94 pounds, while IAG shares fall 1% at 3.77 pounds. Ryanair shares in London slip 0.3%. The FTSE 100 rises 0.4% partly thanks to Diploma's rise after it upgraded its full-year organic growth outlook to 10%. (cristina.gallardo@wsj.com)

0450 ET - EasyJet's third quarter is facing headwinds from later bookings and softer yields, Peel Hunt analysts Alexander Paterson and Ivor Jones say. Looking further ahead, earnings also face a hit from industrial action taken by France's air traffic controllers and slightly higher fuel costs. The analysts expect pretax profit consensus forecasts of about 700 million pounds for fiscal 2025 to fall by a mid-single digit percentage. However, its holidays business continues to progress well, they say, noting that easyJet remains undervalued. Shares are down 6.6% at 4.91 pounds. (cristina.gallardo@wsj.com)

0441 ET - Volvo Car consensus expectations are unlikely to change much on the back of second-quarter earnings, though positive free cash flow is reassuring, UBS analyst David Lesne writes. Sales in the quarter are 7% above Visible Alpha consensus while EBIT excluding joint ventures and items affecting comparability is around 30% above consensus. Second-quarter positive free cash flow of 4.2 billion Swedish kronor compares to UBS's expectation of an outflow. The company booked 1.4 billion kronor of restructuring costs and 11.4 billion kronor of impairment charges, but it is unclear what was reflected in consensus, Lesne says. Production declined by 17% as retail sales fell 12%, suggesting some strong inventory reduction during the quarter, he adds. Shares rise 11%. (dominic.chopping@wsj.com)

0433 ET - EasyJet's holidays business again stole the show for the budget carrier's third quarter, Interactive Investor's Richard Hunter says. This is a burgeoning business that benefits from people becoming more concerned about prices and searching for value packages, he says. The holidays business currently accounts for 15% of EasyJet's total group revenue, but this could grow, he adds. The strike by France's air traffic controllers is the latest of the challenges that hit airlines over the years, ranging from volcanic ash clouds and geopolitical tensions to virus outbreaks, Hunter adds. He notes that EasyJet's share price remains down by 59% from prepandemic levels. Overall, EasyJet delivered a strong quarter that positions the carrier for another profitable year, he says. Shares are down 7.1% at 4.89 pounds. (cristina.gallardo@wsj.com)

0414 ET - EasyJet's revenue per available seat kilometer--a metric that measures an airline's revenue generation efficiency--grew less than expected in the third quarter, says Goodbody's Dudley Shanley. For the period, RASK increased only by 0.5% on year, he notes. This is likely to disappoint investors, Shanley says. Meanwhile, headline pretax profit of 286 million pounds for the third quarter was in line with expectations, he adds. Shares are down 7.5% at 4.87 pounds. (cristina.gallardo@wsj.com)

0412 ET - Volvo Car has delivered on cash preservation in the second quarter, while Ebit and margins have slightly beaten expectations, Jefferies analysts write. There was a deterioration in price/mix during the quarter, but there is a seemingly low tariff impact. New management slammed the brakes on working capital, which has yielded 6.4 billion Swedish kronor of positive free cash flow while maintaining investments, the bank says. There's still no guidance, but management claims the cost turnaround is on track. Shares rise 10%. (dominic.chopping@wsj.com)

0405 ET - Volvo AB's second-quarter results delivered a mixture of ups and downs, RBC Capital Markets analyst Nick Housden says in a research note. Sales at the Swedish vehicle maker's truck unit slightly disappointed, although many investors were braced for softness here, Housden says. However, the construction equipment segment is recovering nicely, he adds. The truck maker leaving its outlook for North American market unchanged was also a pleasant surprise, the analyst adds. Shares trade 0.1% lower at 269.10 kronor. (nina.kienle@wsj.com)

0352 ET - EasyJet confirmed passengers are taking longer than usual to book its summer flights, a trend also flagged by other airlines, J.P. Morgan analysts say. The budget carrier has sold 85% of its seats available for its fourth quarter, so it should have a decent idea by now on its peak summer results, they add. The group's holidays business is growing quickly, and it could meet its medium-term target of 250 million pounds of pretax profit earlier than expected, the analysts say. Shares are down 7.3% at 4.87 pounds. (cristina.gallardo@wsj.com)

0351 ET - Wizz Air's decision to stop its Abu-Dhabi-based operations is a very positive strategic move, Barclays analysts say. It would have made sense for Wizz Air to seek a buyer for its Abu-Dhabi operations, but instead it is closing the business and this will come with costs, including compensation to its joint-venture partner, airports and other suppliers, they add. Redundancy costs may be limited because its United Arab Emirates staff aren't unionized, the analysts say. They would like to see Wizz cut its growth plans to single-digit growth rates from about 20%, and cancel its orders for the A321XLR aircraft, the latest addition to Airbus's A320neo family. The analysts say these planes aren't well suited for low-cost flights. Barclays upgrades its Wizz Air rating to equalweight from underweight. Shares are down 0.3% at 10.16 pounds. (cristina.gallardo@wsj.com)

data.title: One child killed in UK school bus crash
data.description:
British police said on Thursday one child was killed and at least two others seriously injured after a collision involving a bus carrying school children in Somerset, southwest England.

Avon and Somerset Police said the bus was carrying 60-70 people and was heading to Minehead Middle School at the time of the crash.

A number of people were seriously injured, including two children who were taken to hospital by air ambulance, Chief Superintendent Mark Edgington told reporters.

Police declared the collision a major incident, meaning an event with serious consequences requiring special arrangements from emergency services.

Fire crews had earlier rescued people trapped in the bus in what was a "complex and technically difficult incident", Chief Fire Officer for the Devon and Somerset Fire and Rescue Service Gavin Ellis, said.

British Prime Minister Keir Starmer said the incident was "heartbreaking."

"There are no adequate words to acknowledge the death of a child. All my thoughts are with their parents, family and friends, and all those affected," he wrote on X.

data.title: Nvidia Holds Citi Buy Rating, $190 Target Despite Insider Selling
data.description:
Citi kept its Buy recommendation for Nvidia (NVDA, Financials) on Wednesday, with analyst Atif Malik setting a price objective of $190. The stock started at $171.19, which means it might go up roughly 11%.

Citi's projection comes out when Nvidia's price-to-earnings ratio is 53.1 and everyone on Wall Street is positive.

Even while analysts are hopeful, corporate insiders are not. Last month, Director Mark A. Stevens sold 608,248 shares of Nvidia stock for more than $88 million.

Investors may have to reconcile the sustained demand for AI chips against the prudence shown by executives selling their shares, especially because Nvidia is flirting with all-time highs as its value rises.

data.title: Western Alliance Bancorp Q2 Earnings, Revenue Rise
data.description:
Western Alliance Bancorp reported Q2 earnings late Thursday of $2.07 per diluted share, up from $1.75 a year earlier.

Analysts polled by FactSet expected $2.02.

Revenue for the quarter ended June 30, expressed as the sum of net interest income and total noninterest income, was $845.9 million, up from $771.8 million a year earlier.

Analysts surveyed by FactSet expected $838 million.

data.title: CON EDISON DECLARES COMMON STOCK DIVIDEND
data.description:
NEW YORK, July 17, 2025 /PRNewswire/ — Consolidated Edison, Inc. (Con Edison) declared a quarterly dividend of 85 cents a share on its common stock, payable September 15, 2025 to stockholders of record as of August 13, 2025.

Consolidated Edison, Inc. is one of the nation's largest investor-owned energy-delivery companies, with approximately $15 billion in annual revenues for the year ended December 31, 2024 and $71 billion in assets as of March 31, 2025. The company provides a wide range of energy-related products and services to its customers through the following subsidiaries: Consolidated Edison Company of New York, Inc. (CECONY), a regulated utility providing electric service in New York City and New York's Westchester County, gas service in Manhattan, the Bronx, parts of Queens and parts of Westchester, and steam service in Manhattan; Orange and Rockland Utilities, Inc. (O&R), a regulated utility serving customers in a 1,300-square-mile area in southeastern New York State and northern New Jersey; and Con Edison Transmission, Inc., which falls primarily under the oversight of the Federal Energy Regulatory Commission, invests in electric transmission projects and manages, through joint ventures, both electric and gas assets while seeking to develop electric transmission projects.

View original content to download multimedia:https://www.prnewswire.com/news-releases/con-edison-declares-common-stock-dividend-302508323.html

SOURCE Consolidated Edison, Inc.

data.title: Elevance Health Tumbles After Slashing 2025 Outlook on Medicaid Weakness
data.description:
Elevance Health (ELV, Financials) shares fell nearly 12% Thursday morning after the health insurer posted weaker-than-expected Q2 earnings and issued a major downgrade to its full-year profit forecast, citing rising medical costs in its Medicaid and ACA businesses.

While revenue grew a strong 14.3% year over year to $49.42 billion, beating expectations, adjusted earnings came in at $8.84 per share, well below Wall Street's forecast of $9.20.

The big issue: medical costs. The company's benefit expense ratio jumped to 88.9%, up 260 basis points from a year ago, driven by higher claims in Medicaid and Affordable Care Act plans. That pressure outweighed improving efficiency, as the operating expense ratio fell to 10.1%, thanks to disciplined cost controls and revenue leverage.

Total medical membership dropped by 212,000 from Q1, with declines in Medicaid and ACA coverage offsetting gains in Medicare Advantage.

Elevance's Carelon business, which includes home health and pharmacy services, was a bright spot revenues surged 36% to $18.1 billion, helped by acquisitions and strong product performance in CarelonRx.

But that wasn't enough to cushion the blow. The company slashed its full-year EPS guidance to around $30, down from its prior $34.15$34.85 range and well below the Street's $34.40 consensus.

We're adjusting our outlook to reflect what we're seeing in Medicaid and ACA, said CEO Gail Boudreaux, adding that Elevance remains focused on managing healthcare costs and making targeted tech investments to support long-term value.

data.title: Netflix Beats Second-Quarter Estimates Amid Membership, Pricing Gains
data.description:
Netflix's second-quarter results exceeded Wall Street's estimates as the streaming giant's subscriber count grew and it benefited from pricing gains.

Per-share earnings climbed to $7.19 from $4.88 a year earlier, above the GAAP consensus on FactSet for $7.07. Revenue increased 16% year-over-year to $11.08 billion, topping the Street's $11.06 billion view.

Membership growth, price increases, and higher ad revenue helped buoy the second-quarter top line, the streamer said in a letter to shareholders.

Netflix stopped reporting paid membership data quarterly beginning with its earnings report in April.

For 2025, Netflix lifted its revenue guidance to between $44.8 billion and $45.2 billion from $43.5 billion to $44.5 billion expected previously. The analyst consensus is for $44.55 billion.

"The majority of the increase in our revenue forecast reflects the recent depreciation of the US dollar (versus) most other currencies, with the balance attributable to continued business momentum driven by solid member growth and ad sales," the company said.

Netflix projects third-quarter revenue rising 17% year-over-year to $11.53 billion, higher than analysts' $11.28 billion estimate. EPS is pegged at $6.87, versus the consensus view of $6.69.

Shares were down 0.4% in after-hours trade.

data.title: Correction: Uipath Insider Sold Shares Worth $560,142, According to a Recent SEC Filing
data.description:
Daniel Dines, 10% Owner, Director, CEO and Chairman, on July 17, 2025, sold 45,000 shares in Uipath for $560,142. Following the Form 4 filing with the SEC, Dines has control over a total of 30,171,961 Class A common shares of the company, with 24,918,585 shares held directly and 5,253,376 controlled indirectly.

SEC Filing:

www.sec.gov/Archives/edgar/data/1734722/000156218025005453/xslF345X05/primarydocument.xml

data.title: US authors suing Anthropic can band together in copyright class action, judge rules
data.description:
A California federal judge ruled on Thursday that three authors suing artificial intelligence startup Anthropic for copyright infringement can represent writers nationwide whose books Anthropic allegedly pirated to train its AI system.

U.S. District Judge William Alsup said the authors can bring a class action on behalf of all U.S. writers whose works Anthropic allegedly downloaded from "pirate libraries" LibGen and PiLiMi to create a repository of millions of books in 2021 and 2022.

Alsup said Anthropic may have illegally downloaded as many as 7 million books from the pirate websites, which could make it liable for billions of dollars in damages if the authors' case is successful.

An Anthropic spokesperson said the company was considering options to challenge the ruling and that the court failed to account for the difficulty of establishing copyright ownership "millions of times over in a single lawsuit." An attorney for the authors declined to comment on the decision.

Andrea Bartz, Charles Graeber and Kirk Wallace Johnson sued Anthropic last year, arguing that the Amazon- and Alphabet-backed startup used their books without permission or compensation to teach its chatbot Claude to respond to human prompts.

The case is one of several high-stakes lawsuits brought by authors, news outlets and other copyright owners against companies including OpenAI, Microsoft and Meta Platforms over their AI training.

AI companies argue their systems make fair use of copyrighted material to create new, transformative content. Alsup determined in June that Anthropic's AI training made fair use of authors' works, but said the company still violated their rights by saving pirated copies of their books to a "central library of all the books in the world" that would not necessarily be used for AI training.

Alsup said on Thursday the three authors could represent all writers whose books Anthropic allegedly downloaded from LibGen and PiLiMi, rejecting Anthropic's argument that identifying all of the copyright-eligible works and their authors would be impractical.

data.title: WEC Energy Group Maintains Quarterly Dividend at $0.8925 a Share, Payable Sept. 1 to Holders of Record Aug. 14
Slovakia to allow approval of new EU sanctions on Russia on Friday, PM Fico says
data.description:
Slovakia will stop blocking the approval of the 18th package of European Union sanctions against Russia on Friday, Prime Minister Robert Fico said.

Fico said on Thursday Slovakia had achieved as much as it could at this point, after blocking the EU's approval of the sanctions multiple times to demand guarantees against damages it fears from a separate EU plan to end all gas imports from Russia from 2028.

"At this point, it would be counterproductive to continue blocking the 18th sanctions package tomorrow," Fico said in a video message posted on Facebook.

EU countries' ambassadors will meet on Friday morning to approve the new sanctions, EU diplomats told Reuters.

The European Commission last month proposed the 18th package of sanctions against Russia for its 2022 invasion of Ukraine, aimed at Moscow's energy revenue, banks, and military industry.

The proposed package included a floating price cap on Russian oil of 15% below the average market price of crude in the previous three months, EU diplomats have said.

The proposal would also ban transactions with Russia's Nord Stream gas pipelines, as well as banks that engage in sanctions circumvention.

Slovakia has vetoed the package several times to try to win concessions on the separate plan to phase out Russian oil and gas, which, unlike sanctions, does not need unanimous support from EU countries.

Slovakia continues to import Russian energy, including gas under a contract running until 2034, and often takes pro-Russian views on Ukraine.

Fico said on Tuesday that Slovakia had received guarantees from the Commission on assistance in case of potential gas shortages or jumps in prices and transit fees, and assistance in disputes over potential damage claims from Russian supplier Gazprom .

The Commission said in a letter to Slovakia on Tuesday it would intervene in potential litigation, and also clarify how an "emergency break" can be triggered if gas prices spike because of scarce supply during the Russian gas phase-out.

Brussels will also develop a solution that aims to reduce the costs of cross-border tariffs on gas and oil for Slovakia, said the letter.

Malta had also previously expressed reservations about the proposed Russian oil price cap, but the government said on Thursday evening it would also support the new sanctions on Friday, EU diplomats told Reuters.

data.title: AMZN: Amazon Slashes Cloud Staff as Growth Momentum Softens
data.description:
July 17 - Amazon is cutting an undisclosed number of positions in its cloud computing arm, the company confirmed.

Brad Glasser, an Amazon spokesperson, said the decision follows a thorough review of our organization, our priorities, and what we need to focus on going forward, and will affect certain teams within Amazon Web Services. The company declined to specify which units or how many roles will be eliminated.

This move extends a workforce reduction that has seen more than 27,000 job cuts since 2022, even as AWS revenue continues to grow. In the first quarter, AWS sales climbed 17% to $29.27 billion, down slightly from an 18.9% gain in the prior period, underscoring management's push to streamline operations and refocus resources. Reuters first reported the layoffs.

Amazon stressed the cuts are not primarily tied to its generative AI investments and noted it remains open to hiring other roles within AWS. CEO Andy Jassy has warned that AI adoption could reduce our total corporate workforce over the next few years, as some tasks are automated and new skills are demanded.

data.title: WEIS MARKETS ANNOUNCES QUARTERLY DIVIDEND
data.description:
SUNBURY, Pa., July 17, 2025 /PRNewswire/ — Weis Markets, Inc.'s Board of Directors today declared a quarterly dividend of $0.34 per share to shareholders of record as of July 28, 2025, payable on August 11, 2025.

About Weis Markets

Founded in 1912, Weis Markets, Inc. is a Mid-Atlantic food retailer operating 199 stores in Pennsylvania, Maryland, Delaware, New Jersey, New York, Virginia and West Virginia. For more information, please visit: WeisMarkets.com or Facebook.com/WeisMarkets.

View original content to download multimedia:https://www.prnewswire.com/news-releases/weis-markets-announces-quarterly-dividend-302508318.html

SOURCE Weis Markets

data.title: Moody's Ratings Upgrades Argentina's Ratings To Caa1 & Changes Outlook To Stable
data.description:
Moody's:

  • MOODY'S ON ARGENTINA: UPGRADE REFLECTS EXTENSIVE LIBERALIZATION OF EXCHANGE & CAPITAL CONTROLS SUPPORT AVAILABILITY OF HARD CURRENCY LIQUIDITY

  • MOODY'S ON ARGENTINA: WEAK EXTERNAL BUFFERS AND STRUCTURAL IMPEDIMENTS TO INVESTMENT REPRESENT PERSISTENT CHALLENGES TO EXTERNAL STABILITY

data.title: Metallus to Begin Labor Discussions With United Steelworkers in August
data.description:
Metallus said Thursday it will begin labor negotiations with the United Steelworkers on Aug. 18.

The talks concern the current labor contract, which will expire on Sept. 29, the company said.

About 1,200 union workers at Metallus' Canton, Ohio, facility are covered by the agreement, it added.

data.title: Imperial Oil Files 8K - Regulation FD >IMO
data.description:
Imperial Oil Ltd. (IMO) filed a Form 8K - Regulation FD Disclosure - with the U.S Securities and Exchange Commission on July 17, 2025.

On July 17, 2025, Imperial Oil Limited (the "company") by means of a press release advised that a second quarter earnings call will take place at 9:00 a.m. MT on August 1, 2025, following the release of the company's second quarter earnings. A copy of the press release is attached as Exhibit 99.1 to this report.

The full text of this SEC filing can be retrieved at: https://www.sec.gov/Archives/edgar/data/49938/000004993825000039/imo-20250717.htm

Any exhibits and associated documents for this SEC filing can be retrieved at: https://www.sec.gov/Archives/edgar/data/49938/000004993825000039/0000049938-25-000039-index.htm

Public companies must file a Form 8-K, or current report, with the SEC generally within four days of any event that could materially affect a company's financial position or the value of its shares.

data.title: First Financial Bankshares Inc reports results for the quarter ended June 30 - Earnings Summary
data.description:
• First Financial Bankshares Inc reported quarterly adjusted earnings of 47 cents​​ per share for the quarter ended June 30, higher than the same quarter last year, when the company reported EPS of 37 cents. The mean expectation of four analysts for the quarter was for earnings of 44 cents per share. Wall Street expected results to range from 43 cents to 45 cents per share.

  • Revenue rose 16.4% to $156.60 million from a year ago; analysts expected $156.41 million.

  • First Financial Bankshares Inc's reported EPS for the quarter was 47 cents​.

  • The company reported quarterly net income of $66.66 million.

  • First Financial Bankshares Inc shares had risen by 0.9% this quarter and gained 0.7% so far this year.

FORECAST CHANGES

  • The mean earnings estimate of analysts had risen by about 0.8% in the last three months.​

  • In the last 30 days, there have been no earnings estimate revisions by analysts covering the company.

RECOMMENDATIONS

  • The current average analyst rating on the shares is "hold" and the breakdown of recommendations is no "strong buy" or "buy," 4 "hold" and no "sell" or "strong sell." The average consensus recommendation for the banks peer group is "buy."

  • Wall Street's median 12-month price target for First Financial Bankshares Inc is $36.50, about 0.5% above its last closing price of $36.32

This summary was machine generated from LSEG data July 17 at 08:37 p.m. UTC. ​All figures in US dollars unless otherwise stated. data.title: Uipath Insider Sold Shares Worth $560,142, According to a Recent SEC Filing
data.description:
Daniel Dines, 10% Owner, Director, CEO and Chairman, on July 17, 2025, sold 45,000 shares in Uipath for $560,142. Following the Form 4 filing with the SEC, Dines has control over a total of 30,171,961 shares of the company, with 24,918,585 shares held directly and 5,253,376 controlled indirectly.

SEC Filing:
